en.extract:
The black-headed woodpecker (Picus erythropygius) is a species of bird in the family Picidae. It typically inhabits deciduous and coniferous forests and is found in Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam.


Description

The black-headed woodpecker is a medium-sized bird at around 33 cm tall and 100-135g. Adult birds have very distinct plumage and have a black face, yellow throat, green wings, a red lower back, and a white stomach. They also have white/yellow eyes and some individuals have a thin white line from their eye to their neck. Males have a red crown, while females have a completely black crown with no red. Juveniles are less pigmented than adults and juvenile males have fewer red crown feathers on their heads.


Taxonomy
The black-headed woodpecker is a member of the woodpecker family Picidae and the genus Picus. They have been considered to be most closely related to the European green woodpecker (P. viridis) and the grey-headed woodpecker (P. canus). However, recent studies have challenged this original phylogenetic relationship.


Subspecies
There are two recognized subspecies of the black-headed woodpecker:

Picus erythropygius nigrigenis (Hume, 1874) – Myanmar and Northwest-West Thailand. This subspecies is more commonly found and can be identified by its black bill.
Picus erythropygius erythropygius (Elliot, 1865) – Northeast Thailand and Indochina. This subspecies has a white bill, as opposed to a black one.


Distribution and habitat
Black-headed woodpeckers are found in Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam. They are most commonly found in the mid-story and sub-canopy of deciduous and coniferous forests. They usually prefer dry dipterocarp forests, semi-evergreen forests, riverine forests, and savanna forests. They are also resident birds.


Behaviour and ecology


Diet

Black-headed woodpeckers, like most woodpeckers, are insectivorous and glean insects on the bark of trees. They most commonly consume termites, but they also eat ants and other insects and invertebrates.


Vocalization
Their song is a loud series of yelps that sound similar to laughing. These yelps ("ka-tek-a-tek-a-tek-a-tek" or "cha-cha-cha, cha-cha-cha") are rapidly repeated and the first note is emphasized.


Reproduction
Little is known about their reproduction, but they reproduce between February–June and nest within trees. They also have around 3-4 eggs. In a wildlife sanctuary in Chiang Mai, their nests were occupied in May.


Flocks
Black-headed woodpeckers are usually found in groups with other bird species. These bird waves consist of larger flock sizes, which allow birds to worry less about predators and spend more time foraging. Black-headed woodpeckers are typically found in flocks with white-crested laughingthrushes (Garrulax leucolophus), lesser necklaced laughingthrushes (Garrulax monileger), and greater racket-tailed drongos (Dicrurus paradiseus). In these flocks, black-headed woodpeckers, along with the other birds, produce alarm calls when predators approach.


Parasites
The black-headed woodpecker is vulnerable to many parasites, including avian haemoproteid parasite, Haemoproteus bennetti, which has been detected in its blood. They are also susceptible to Picidae-specific parasites, including chewing lice, Picicola roberti, and quill mites, Picobia heeri, which inhabit the feather quills.


Status and Conservation
The black-headed woodpecker is currently classified as least concern, but populations are decreasing. This is mostly due to habitat destruction caused by human settlements and agricultural fields.
In 2003, the black-headed woodpecker was declared a protected wildlife species by the Thai government. The species is also found in many wildlife sanctuaries across Thailand.


References


External links
Black-headed woodpecker audio. Macaulay Library – The Cornell Lab of Ornithology.

The '''black-headed woodpecker''' ('''''Picus erythropygius''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Picidae]]. It typically inhabits [[deciduous]] and [[conifer]]ous forests and is found in [[Cambodia]], [[Laos]], [[Myanmar]], [[Thailand]], and [[Vietnam]].

== Description ==
{{Commons}}
[[File:GecinusErythropygiusSmit.jpg|thumb|Female and male black-headed woodpeckers]]
The black-headed woodpecker is a medium-sized bird at around 33 cm tall and 100-135g.<ref name=":0">{{Citation|last1=Winkler|first1=Hans|title=Black-headed Woodpecker (Picus erythropygius)|date=2020-03-04|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/blhwoo1/1.0/introduction|work=Birds of the World|editor-last=Billerman|editor-first=Shawn M.|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|language=en|doi=10.2173/bow.blhwoo1.01|access-date=2021-11-10|last2=Christie|first2=David|s2cid=216205008 |editor2-last=Keeney|editor2-first=Brooke K.|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor3-first=Paul G.|editor4-last=Schulenberg|editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Adult birds have very distinct plumage and have a black face, yellow throat, green wings, a red lower back, and a white stomach.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Black-headed Woodpecker – eBird|url=https://ebird.org/species/blhwoo1|access-date=2021-11-10|website=ebird.org|language=en}}</ref> They also have white/yellow eyes and some individuals have a thin white line from their eye to their neck.<ref name=":0" /> Males have a red crown, while females have a completely black crown with no red.<ref name=":0" /> Juveniles are less pigmented than adults and juvenile males have fewer red crown feathers on their heads.<ref name=":0" />

== Taxonomy ==
The black-headed woodpecker is a member of the woodpecker family Picidae and the genus [[Picus (bird)|Picus]]. They have been considered to be most closely related to the [[European green woodpecker]] (''P. viridis'') and the [[grey-headed woodpecker]] (''P. canus'').<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Fuchs|first1=Jérôme|last2=Pons|first2=Jean-Marc|last3=Ericson|first3=Per G.P.|last4=Bonillo|first4=Céline|last5=Couloux|first5=Arnaud|last6=Pasquet|first6=Eric|date=2008|title=Molecular support for a rapid cladogenesis of the woodpecker clade Malarpicini, with further insights into the genus Picus (Piciformes: Picinae)|url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1055790308001462|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|language=en|volume=48|issue=1|pages=34–46|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2008.03.036|pmid=18487062 |bibcode=2008MolPE..48...34F |url-access=subscription}}</ref> However, recent studies have challenged this original phylogenetic relationship.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Pons|first1=J.-M.|last2=Olioso|first2=G.|last3=Cruaud|first3=C.|last4=Fuchs|first4=J.|date=2010-10-05|title=Phylogeography of the Eurasian green woodpecker (Picus viridis)|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1365-2699.2010.02401.x|journal=Journal of Biogeography|language=en|volume=38|issue=2|pages=311–325|doi=10.1111/j.1365-2699.2010.02401.x|s2cid=85704825 |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
There are two recognized subspecies of the black-headed woodpecker:<ref>{{Cite web|title=Picus erythropygius|url=https://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=554124#null|url-status=live|access-date=2021-11-10|website=ITIS – Integrated Taxonomic Information System|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111023000645/http://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=554124 |archive-date=23 October 2011 }}</ref>

* ''Picus erythropygius nigrigenis'' ([[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]], 1874) – Myanmar and Northwest-West Thailand.<ref name=":0" /> This subspecies is more commonly found and can be identified by its black bill.<ref name=":0" />
* ''Picus erythropygius erythropygius'' ([[Daniel Giraud Elliot|Elliot]], 1865) – Northeast Thailand and [[Mainland Southeast Asia|Indochina]].<ref name=":0" /> This subspecies has a white bill, as opposed to a black one.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=de Schauensee|first=Rodolphe Meyer|date=1946|title=On Siamese Birds|journal=Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia|volume=98|pages=1–82|jstor=4064390 |issn=0097-3157}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
Black-headed woodpeckers are found in Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" /> They are most commonly found in the mid-story and sub-canopy of deciduous and coniferous forests.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Mittermeier|first1=John C.|last2=Sandvig|first2=Erik M.|last3=Jocque|first3=Merlijn|date=2019|title=Surveys in 2018 along the Mekong River, northern Kratie province, Cambodia, indicate a decade of declines in populations of threatened bird species|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/340417604|journal=BirdingASIA|volume=32|pages=80–89}}</ref> They usually prefer dry [[Dipterocarpaceae|dipterocarp]] forests, semi-[[evergreen]] forests, riverine forests, and [[savanna]] forests.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last=Stott|first=Philip|date=1984-09-01|title=The savanna forests of mainland southeast Asia: an ecological survey|url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/030913338400800301|journal=Progress in Physical Geography: Earth and Environment|language=en|volume=8|issue=3|pages=315–335|doi=10.1177/030913338400800301|s2cid=130984062 |issn=0309-1333|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Zusi|first1=R. L.|last2=Marshall|first2=J. T.|date=1970|title=A comparison of Asiatic and North American sapsuckers|url=http://www.siamese-heritage.org/nhbsspdf/vol021-030/NHBSS_023_3q_Zusi_AComparisonOfAsiatic.pdf|journal=Natural History Bulletin of the Siam Society|volume=23|pages=393–407}}</ref> They are also resident birds.<ref name=":0" />

== Behaviour and ecology ==

=== Diet ===
[[File:Black-headed Woodpecker.jpg|thumb|Black-headed woodpecker on a tree]]
Black-headed woodpeckers, like most woodpeckers, are [[Insectivore|insectivorous]] and glean [[insect]]s on the bark of trees.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Limparungpatthanakij|first1=Wichyanan|last2=Gale|first2=George|last3=Brockelman|first3=Warren Y.|last4=Round|first4=Philip David|date=2017|title=Western striped squirrel Tamiops mcclellandii: A non-avian sentinel species of bird waves|url=http://zoobank.org/References/4E7F86EB-4692-41F9-8039-8E17B479AA8D|journal=Raffles Bulletin of Zoology|volume=65|pages=474–481|archive-date=22 January 2022|access-date=11 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122070732/http://zoobank.org/References/4E7F86EB-4692-41F9-8039-8E17B479AA8D|url-status=dead}}</ref> They most commonly consume [[termite]]s, but they also eat [[ant]]s and other insects and [[invertebrate]]s.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":5" />

=== Vocalization ===
Their song is a loud series of yelps that sound similar to laughing.<ref name=":1" /> These yelps ("ka-tek-a-tek-a-tek-a-tek" or "cha-cha-cha, cha-cha-cha") are rapidly repeated and the first note is emphasized.<ref name=":0" />

=== Reproduction ===
Little is known about their reproduction, but they reproduce between February–June and nest within trees.<ref name=":0" /> They also have around 3-4 eggs.<ref name=":0" /> In a wildlife sanctuary in [[Chiang Mai]], their nests were occupied in May.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Round|first=Philip D.|date=1982|title=Notes on Breeding Birds in North-West Thailand|url=https://thesiamsociety.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/NHBSS_030_1d_Round_NotesOnBreedingBird.pdf|journal=Nat. Hist. Bull. Siam Soc.|volume=30|pages=1–14}}</ref>

=== Flocks ===
Black-headed woodpeckers are usually found in groups with other bird species.<ref name=":0" /> These [[Mixed-species foraging flock|bird waves]] consist of larger flock sizes, which allow birds to worry less about predators and spend more time foraging.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Limparungpatthanakij|first1=Wichyanan|last2=Brockelman|first2=Warren Y|last3=Gale|first3=George|last4=Round|first4=Philip David|date=2019|title=Woodpeckers benefit from participation in mixed-species flocks in lowland deciduous forests, Huai Kha Khaeng Wildlife Sanctuary, Thailand|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/342349219|journal=Forktail|volume=35|pages=57–59}}</ref> Black-headed woodpeckers are typically found in flocks with [[white-crested laughingthrush]]es (''Garrulax leucolophus''), [[lesser necklaced laughingthrush]]es (''Garrulax'' ''monileger''), and [[greater racket-tailed drongo]]s (''Dicrurus paradiseus'').<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6" /> In these flocks, black-headed woodpeckers, along with the other birds, produce alarm calls when predators approach.<ref name=":5" />

=== Parasites ===
The black-headed woodpecker is vulnerable to many parasites, including avian [[Haemoproteidae|haemoproteid]] parasite, ''Haemoproteus bennetti'', which has been detected in its blood.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Greiner|first1=Ellis C|last2=Mandal|first2=A. K.|last3=Nandi|first3=N. C.|date=1977|title=Haemoproteus bennetti sp. n. and a Review of the Haemoproteids from the Picidae (Woodpeckers)|journal=The Journal of Parasitology|volume=63|issue=4|pages=651–656|doi=10.2307/3279563 |jstor=3279563 |pmid=407347 }}</ref> They are also susceptible to Picidae-specific parasites, including [[Ischnocera|chewing lice]], ''Picicola roberti'', and [[Syringophilidae|quill mites]], ''Picobia heeri,'' which inhabit the [[feather]] quills.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Somadder|first1=K.|last2=Tandan|first2=B.K.|date=1975|title=Chewing lice from birds of the oriental region. Part XI: Picicola Roberti, sp. nov. (Phthiraptera: Ischnocera)|url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/00305316.1975.10434840|journal=Oriental Insects|language=en|volume=9|issue=1|pages=23–30|doi=10.1080/00305316.1975.10434840|issn=0030-5316|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Zmudzinski|first1=Mateusz|last2=Unsoeld|first2=Markus|date=2019-03-11|title=Quill mites (Acariformes: Syringophilidae) parasitizing birds in Germany: new host records and descriptions of two new species from Limosa lapponica (L.) (Aves: Scolopacidae)|url=https://biotaxa.org/saa/article/view/saa.24.3.3|journal=Systematic and Applied Acarology|volume=24|issue=3|pages=362–376|doi=10.11158/saa.24.3.3|s2cid=92799823 |issn=1362-1971|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

== Status and Conservation ==
The black-headed woodpecker is currently classified as least concern, but populations are decreasing.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" /> This is mostly due to [[habitat destruction]] caused by human settlements and agricultural fields.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Treesucon|first1=Uthai|last2=Round|first2=Philip D.|date=1990|title=Report on Threatened Birds in Thailand|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/39014374|journal=Tiger Paper|volume=17|issue=3|pages=1–9}}</ref>

In 2003, the black-headed woodpecker was declared a protected wildlife species by the Thai government.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2003|title=กฎกระทรวง ก ำหนดให้สัตว์ป่ำบางชนิดเป็นสัตว์ป่ำคุ้มครอง พ.ศ. ๒๕๔๖|url=https://www4.fisheries.go.th/local/file_document/20200410114550_new.pdf|url-status=live|access-date=2021-11-10|website=กรมประมง (Department of Fisheries, Thailand)|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211111023650/https://www4.fisheries.go.th/local/file_document/20200410114550_new.pdf |archive-date=11 November 2021 }}</ref> The species is also found in many wildlife sanctuaries across Thailand.<ref>{{Cite web|title=นกหัวขวานเขียวตะโพกแดง Black-headed Woodpecker ( Picus erythropygius (Elliot, 1865) )|url=http://www.lowernorthernbird.com/checklist.php?cat_id=18&c_id=54&b_id=39|url-status=live|access-date=2021-11-11|website=Birds of the Lower Northern Thailand|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211111023654/http://www.lowernorthernbird.com/checklist.php?cat_id=18&c_id=54&b_id=39 |archive-date=11 November 2021 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Wiles|first=Gary J.|date=1979|title=The Birds of Salak Phra Wildlife Sanctuary, Southwestern Thailand|url=http://www.siamese-heritage.org/nhbsspdf/vol021-030/NHBSS_028_i_Wiles_TheBirdsOfSalakPhra.pdf|journal=Nat. Hist. Bull. Siam Soc.|volume=28|pages=101–120}}</ref>
